content.text: The ElizaOS token saw a major price surge early in the week, with community members reporting gains of 90% to 130% from recent lows. Some members took partial profits while others held positions. Community sentiment was broadly optimistic, with members citing praise from major AI platforms including ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude for the solution architecture and GitHub codebase. Features highlighted included scalability to hundreds of thousands of agents, fee optimization, security, and UI/UX improvements. Shaw commented that significant progress had been made.

Shaw made several key announcements during the week. ElizaOS and DegenAI were added as the default payment method in elizaOS for x402 routes. ElizaCloud was consolidated into the main eliza repository on GitHub, migrated away from Vercel, and updated to support cheaper container infrastructure, allowing users to build and monetize their own apps on top of it. Shaw also clarified that elizaOS is a fair launch with no VC backing and no formal team structure, explaining the project had to shut down its team model due to lack of profit and now consists of builders using and building the technology.

Shaw confirmed active development work on a model called eliza-1, described as a general purpose model based on qwen but trained within the Eliza harness. Optimization techniques being applied include dflash speculative decoding, caveman compression on thinking, and turboquant. A beta release labeled v2.0.0-beta.0 was also pushed to GitHub during the week.

Multiple scam incidents were reported across the discussion and coders channels throughout the week. Users warned others about scam links related to ai16z to ElizaOS migration and bridging ElizaOS from BSC to Solana. A legitimate answer to the bridging question was provided by satsbased, who suggested using Wormhole or deBridge. Additional scam flags were raised on May 9 and May 10 in the coders channel.

Odilitime confirmed in the coders channel that the X (Twitter) API is now required for Twitter integration, replacing the previous login method. Pricing has been recently revised and is now much cheaper than before. Separately, Odilitime also confirmed that the cost of running an Eliza agent as a Twitter bot has dropped from approximately 100 dollars per month to around 10 dollars per month, depending on configuration choices such as number of replies.

A user named BinaryCookies reported issues with a missing plugin on GitHub, noting that the elizaOS core did not match the plugin and that it had been removed from the repository. Odilitime shared a link to the eliza-3d-hyperfy-starter plugin but the link returned a 404 error, and the plugin zip was sent directly via direct message. Stan also alerted jin that the elizaOS GitHub summary page at elizaos.github.io/summary/day had been stuck since May 4th, with Odilitime speculating the issue might be related to the GitHub account.

A community member raised observations about ElizaOS coin market behavior, noting that the token has shown positive net fund flow every week since launch, meaning more dollar volume comes from buying at higher prices than selling at lower ones. Despite this, the price continues to fall, leading the member to speculate that an entity, possibly an AI, is strategically selling when the cost of price impact is low and buying when large sell volume is available.

On May 4, 2026, ElizaOS expanded cloud infrastructure and monetization capabilities. Support was enabled for monetized container app domains including route ownership and Cloudflare management. System stability was improved by resolving billing data fetch loops and consolidating Pages Functions. The Cloud dashboard and CLI login flows were stabilized through fixes to multiple authentication and session synchronization issues.

Plugin and workflow enhancements on May 4 included the migration of the plugin-slack package into the monorepo, centralizing service and action definitions. The n8n workflow generation system was refined with improved prompt rules, structured clarification requests, and better JSON response handling. UI support for inline clarification quick-picks was added to the AutomationsView component. New LifeOps modules were introduced covering proactive planning, inbox management, health monitoring, and activity reporting.

Several new issues were identified on May 4, including agents incorrectly reporting a lack of task and memory tools, the Telegram wrapper bypassing agent runtime and preventing skill and action dispatch, the dangerously-skip-permissions flag failing to bypass confirmation loops, and activity panel flickering under high-volume WebSocket events.

On May 7, 2026, development activity focused on infrastructure and configuration improvements for the ElizaOS documentation site. A new pull request was submitted to the elizaos.github.io repository proposing the addition of an ImportMetaENV interface to the next.config.js file to improve environment variable handling. No technical tasks were completed and no issues were closed across any repositories on this date.

On May 8, 2026, development activity centered on improving security documentation for the DeFi plugin ecosystem. A pull request, elizaos/docs#84, was opened to add SafeAgent, a token safety tool, to the DeFi plugin registry and is currently awaiting review before the integration can be finalized.
